title: Getting Started
text: package ships synthetic dataset, erglm_data, used throughout documentation. exposure metric (aucss), several covariates (sex, age, weight, dose, …), response columns supported family: binary (ae1, ae2), count (ae_count), continuous (biomarker_change), positive right-skewed continuous (ae_duration):
code: erglm_data #> # A tibble: 300 × 13 #>       id sex      age weight  dose treatment aucss cmaxss   ae1   ae2 ae_count #>    <int> <fct>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <fct>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <int> #>  1     1 Male      35     79   200 Drug       673.   97.3     0     1        1 #>  2     2 Female    22     58   200 Drug      2806.  301.      1     1        6 #>  3     3 Female    28     58     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        1 #>  4     4 Female    18     57   100 Drug      1169.  198.      1     1        0 #>  5     5 Male      28     77   100 Drug       377.   51.4     0     0        0 #>  6     6 Female    19     76   200 Drug       327.   25.4     1     0        0 #>  7     7 Male      30     70     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        0 #>  8     8 Female    34     60   100 Drug      1208.  133.      1     1        1 #>  9     9 Male      21     89     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        0 #> 10    10 Female    34     56   200 Drug       254.   31.0     0     0        1 #> # ℹ 290 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: biomarker_change <dbl>, ae_duration <dbl>

title: Getting Started
text: erglm_model() fits model – takes formula/data arguments glm(), plus family argument (defaulting gaussian(), matching glm()’s default):
code: mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss      sexMale   #>   -1.648112     0.005508    -0.312232   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  297 Residual #> Null Deviance:       402.1  #> Residual Deviance: 192.7     AIC: 198.7

title: Getting Started
text: erglm_predict() produces predictions confidence intervals, link response scales, tidy data frame:

title: Getting Started
text: several candidate covariates, erglm_scm_forward() erglm_scm_backward() automate process deciding belong model, via stepwise addition/elimination based significance testing: See “Stepwise covariate modelling” article full treatment, including forward/backward workflow audit log (erglm_scm_history()).
code: erglm_scm_forward(mod, candidates = c("dose", "weight", "age"), seed = 1024) #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss      sexMale   #>   -1.648112     0.005508    -0.312232   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  297 Residual #> Null Deviance:       402.1  #> Residual Deviance: 192.7     AIC: 198.7

title: Getting Started
text: simulate() generates replicate datasets fitted model, erglm_vpc_sim() reshapes replicates data set ready visual predictive check: See “Simulation” article details, including lower-level erglm_fun() building block.
code: erglm_vpc_sim(mod, nsim = 5, seed = 2048) #> # A tibble: 1,500 × 5 #>      ae1 aucss sex    row_id sim_id #>    <int> <dbl> <fct>   <int>  <int> #>  1     0  673. Male        1      1 #>  2     1 2806. Female      2      1 #>  3     0    0  Female      3      1 #>  4     1 1169. Female      4      1 #>  5     1  377. Male        5      1 #>  6     1  327. Female      6      1 #>  7     0    0  Male        7      1 #>  8     1 1208. Female      8      1 #>  9     0    0  Male        9      1 #> 10     1  254. Female     10      1 #> # ℹ 1,490 more rows

title: Getting Started
text: object returned erglm_model() genuine glm object – class c("erglm_model", "glm", "lm") – standard glm/lm methods (summary(), predict(), confint(), AIC(), anova(), ) work directly, erglm-specific replacement needed. See “Using base R model methods” article worked-tour .

title: Getting Started
text: “Modelling” – fitting prediction, including glm() families erglm supports. “Stepwise covariate modelling” – forward/backward SCM workflow full. “Using base R model methods” – glm/lm methods come free. “Simulation” – simulate(), erglm_fun(), erglm_vpc_sim(). companion erplots package, visualising exposure-response models fitted erglm.

title: Using base R model methods
text: summary() gives usual coefficient table, standard errors, dispersion information:
code: summary(mod) #>  #> Call: #> stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #>               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     #> (Intercept) -1.6827031  0.3205633  -5.249 1.53e-07 *** #> aucss        0.0052806  0.0009409   5.612 2.00e-08 *** #> dose         0.0010573  0.0031778   0.333    0.739     #> sexMale     -0.3053683  0.3656515  -0.835    0.404     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 402.13  on 299  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance: 192.56  on 296  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 200.56 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 7

title: Using base R model methods
text: fitted coefficients variance-covariance matrix available via coef() vcov(), exactly glm: Confidence intervals individual coefficients can obtained confint() (profile-likelihood based, slightly slower generally preferred Wald interval glm models):

title: Using base R model methods
text: Base R’s predict() works directly erglm model. default returns predictions link scale; use type = "response" response scale: predict() can also return standard errors (se.fit = TRUE), basis erglm’s [erglm_predict()] – function thin, opinionated wrapper back-transforms link-scale standard errors confidence interval response scale returns everything tidy data frame bound newdata: Use whichever convenient: predict() quick point estimate, erglm_predict() want interval bounds without computing hand.
code: predict(mod, newdata = erglm_data[1:5, ], type = "response") #>         1         2         3         4         5  #> 0.8554138 0.9999984 0.1567379 0.9900114 0.5274632 erglm_predict(mod, newdata = erglm_data[1:5, ]) #> # A tibble: 5 × 18 #>      id sex      age weight  dose treatment aucss cmaxss   ae1   ae2 ae_count #>   <int> <fct>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <fct>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <int> #> 1     1 Male      35     79   200 Drug       673.   97.3     0     1        1 #> 2     2 Female    22     58   200 Drug      2806.  301.      1     1        6 #> 3     3 Female    28     58     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        1 #> 4     4 Female    18     57   100 Drug      1169.  198.      1     1        0 #> 5     5 Male      28     77   100 Drug       377.   51.4     0     0        0 #> # ℹ 7 more variables: biomarker_change <dbl>, ae_duration <dbl>, #> #   fit_link <dbl>, se_link <dbl>, fit_resp <dbl>, ci_lower <dbl>, #> #   ci_upper <dbl>

title: Using base R model methods
text: AIC(), BIC(), logLik() work usual, convenient comparing candidate models outside erglm’s stepwise covariate modelling (erglm_scm_forward()/erglm_scm_backward()): nested models, anova() gives likelihood-ratio (F, families estimated dispersion) test directly – exactly machinery erglm_scm_forward()/erglm_scm_backward() use internally, exposed one-comparisons:
code: mod_no_sex <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + dose, erglm_data, family = binomial())  AIC(mod, mod_no_sex) #>            df      AIC #> mod         4 200.5607 #> mod_no_sex  3 199.2614 BIC(mod, mod_no_sex) #>            df      BIC #> mod         4 215.3758 #> mod_no_sex  3 210.3728 anova(mod_no_sex, mod, test = "Chisq") #> Analysis of Deviance Table #>  #> Model 1: ae1 ~ aucss + dose #> Model 2: ae1 ~ aucss + dose + sex #>   Resid. Df Resid. Dev Df Deviance Pr(>Chi) #> 1       297     193.26                      #> 2       296     192.56  1   0.7007   0.4025

title: Using base R model methods
text: Base R’s plot.lm() diagnostic plots (plot(mod, = 1:4)) also work, though panels (e.g. leverage) informative continuous responses binary ones. erglm deliberately doesn’t provide diagnostic plotting – see companion erplots package exposure-response-specific visualisations.

title: Modelling
text: Creating model:
code: mod <- erglm_model(formula = ae1 ~ aucss, data = erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss   #>   -1.791383     0.005497   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:       402.1  #> Residual Deviance: 193.4     AIC: 197.4

title: Modelling
text: erglm_predict() function produces model predictions: pred reports link-scale (fit_link) response-scale (fit_resp) predictions. need move two scales – e.g. transform raw response-scale value onto link scale – erglm_link() erglm_invlink() extract relevant functions straight model’s glm() family: confidence level can adjusted using conf_level argument Choosing covariates belong model – stepwise covariate modelling erglm_scm_forward()/erglm_scm_backward() – covered article, “Stepwise covariate modelling”.

title: Modelling
text: erglm_data also includes count response (ae_count), continuous response (biomarker_change), right-skewed positive continuous response (ae_duration), demonstrating poisson, gaussian, Gamma models respectively: erglm_predict() erglm_fun() work unchanged, since operate link scale generically via stats::family(object)$linkinv: Stepwise covariate modelling also generalises across families – see “Stepwise covariate modelling” article details.
code: mod_pois <- erglm_model(ae_count ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = poisson()) mod_pois #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss      sexMale   #>   -0.930026     0.001053    -0.172638   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  297 Residual #> Null Deviance:       868.8  #> Residual Deviance: 272.4     AIC: 712.6 mod_pois |>    erglm_predict(newdata = tibble(aucss = seq(0, 3000, by = 500), sex = "Female")) #> # A tibble: 7 × 7 #>   aucss sex    fit_link se_link fit_resp ci_lower ci_upper #>   <dbl> <chr>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1     0 Female   -0.930  0.104     0.395    0.322    0.484 #> 2   500 Female   -0.404  0.0888    0.668    0.561    0.795 #> 3  1000 Female    0.123  0.0769    1.13     0.973    1.31  #> 4  1500 Female    0.650  0.0696    1.91     1.67     2.19  #> 5  2000 Female    1.18   0.0684    3.24     2.84     3.71  #> 6  2500 Female    1.70   0.0736    5.49     4.75     6.34  #> 7  3000 Female    2.23   0.0841    9.29     7.88    11.0

title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: lowest level, covariate modelling just adding removing single term fitted model. Two exported helpers exactly , erglm_add_term() erglm_remove_term(), taking one-sided formula naming term returning refitted model: stepwise functions built top : repeatedly propose add/remove moves, compare candidate current model significance testing (anova()), keep best one. rarely need call erglm_add_term()/erglm_remove_term() directly, ’s worth knowing ’re automated search hood.
code: base_mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = binomial())  erglm_add_term(base_mod, ~ sex) #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss      sexMale   #>    -1.64811      0.00551     -0.31223   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  297 Residual #> Null Deviance:       402  #> Residual Deviance: 193   AIC: 199

title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: Two ingredients needed drive SCM run: base model start , character vector candidate covariates consider. Unlike covariate-modelling frameworks, candidates don’t need tied particular structural parameter – ’re just term names added (removed ) right-hand side model formula:
code: candidates <- c("sex", "dose", "weight", "age")

title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: Every candidate already model added turn compared current model; term smallest pp-value retained, provided pp-value threshold. step repeats remaining candidate clears bar: None four candidates clear default 0.010.01 threshold – inspecting history shows : sex genuine (modest) effect ae1 data-generating process behind erglm_data, aucss already model effect isn’t strong enough, sample size, clear 0.010.01 threshold – search correctly leaves rather adding covariate doesn’t earn place. feature, limitation: SCM exactly tool stop including underpowered terms.
code: fwd_mod <- erglm_scm_forward(base_mod, candidates, threshold = 0.01, seed = 3425) fwd_mod$formula #> ae1 ~ aucss erglm_scm_history(fwd_mod)[, c("term_tested", "term_p_value", "model_updated")] #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   term_tested term_p_value model_updated #>   <chr>              <dbl>         <int> #> 1 NA               NA                 NA #> 2 ~sex              0.391              0 #> 3 ~weight           0.0622             0 #> 4 ~age              0.177              0 #> 5 ~dose             0.702              0

title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: Backward elimination works reverse: term currently model dropped turn, term largest pp-value removed pp-value exceeds threshold. see real work, start saturated model already contains every candidate: four candidates pruned, leaving aucss – matching forward addition already told us: none sex, dose, weight, age earns place model thresholds. full audit log shows iteration--iteration elimination: iteration removes single worst-performing term (model_updated == 1); next iteration re-tests everything still model, since removing one term can change others’ apparent significance. thresholds differ. ’s standard practice make forward addition permissive backward elimination – 0.010.01 add 0.0010.001 retain. term added looser forward criterion must survive stricter backward criterion, guards terms looked useful presence others. Keeping backward threshold forward threshold also prevents procedure cycling (adding removing term forever). Reproducibility. Within step, candidate terms tested random order, results can depend state random number generator; passing seed makes run reproducible. seed omitted, random one chosen reported via message.
code: full_mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + sex + dose + weight + age, erglm_data, family = binomial()) bwd_mod <- erglm_scm_backward(full_mod, candidates, threshold = 0.001, seed = 9821) bwd_mod$formula #> ae1 ~ aucss #> attr(,"variables") #> list(ae1, aucss) #> attr(,"factors") #>       aucss #> ae1       0 #> aucss     1 #> attr(,"term.labels") #> [1] "aucss" #> attr(,"order") #> [1] 1 #> attr(,"intercept") #> [1] 1 #> attr(,"response") #> [1] 1 #> attr(,".Environment") #> <environment: R_GlobalEnv> #> attr(,"predvars") #> list(ae1, aucss) #> attr(,"dataClasses") #>       ae1     aucss  #> "numeric" "numeric" print(   erglm_scm_history(bwd_mod)[, c("iteration", "term_tested", "term_p_value", "model_updated")],   n = Inf ) #> # A tibble: 11 × 4 #>    iteration term_tested term_p_value model_updated #>        <int> <chr>              <dbl>         <int> #>  1         0 NA               NA                 NA #>  2         1 ~sex              0.863              1 #>  3         1 ~dose             0.845              0 #>  4         1 ~age              0.123              0 #>  5         1 ~weight           0.0713             0 #>  6         2 ~weight           0.0442             0 #>  7         2 ~age              0.123              0 #>  8         2 ~dose             0.851              1 #>  9         3 ~age              0.118              1 #> 10         3 ~weight           0.0431             0 #> 11         4 ~weight           0.0622             1

what: Forward addition piped to backward elimination
title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: typical workflow forward-backward run: forward addition build model , immediately followed backward elimination prune . functions take fitted model first argument return fitted model, compose naturally pipe: Since forward addition already found nothing worth adding, backward elimination nothing left prune, pipeline settles base model. final_mod ordinary fitted model object, usual methods apply – summary(), confint(), predict(), behave normally.
code: final_mod <- base_mod |>   erglm_scm_forward(candidates, threshold = 0.01, seed = 3425) |>   erglm_scm_backward(candidates, threshold = 0.001, seed = 9821)  final_mod$formula #> ae1 ~ aucss

title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: Nothing SCM interface changes across glm() families. candidates, applied Poisson-distributed ae_count response, tell analogous story – sex borderline effect (visible full-model summary p≈0.07p \approx 0.07) still doesn’t clear default forward threshold: Internally, erglm_scm_forward()/erglm_scm_backward() pick likelihood-ratio chi-squared test families known dispersion (binomial, poisson, used ) F-test families estimated dispersion parameter (gaussian, Gamma), matching stats::anova()’s test argument – set test argument explicitly override .
code: base_pois <- erglm_model(ae_count ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = poisson()) fwd_pois <- erglm_scm_forward(base_pois, candidates, threshold = 0.01, seed = 3425) fwd_pois$formula #> ae_count ~ aucss

title: Stepwise covariate modelling
text: Selection criterion. current implementation selects pp-values . history records model_aic model_bic every candidate, can audit search information criteria even though aren’t used drive . Threshold choice. forward backward thresholds main levers control. Stricter thresholds yield sparser models. Greediness. Stepwise search greedy isn’t guaranteed find globally best subset covariates – , article’s example shows, may correctly find candidate covariates belong model given sample size threshold. audit log valuable precisely makes path search took transparent reproducible.

title: Simulation
text: Calling simulate() generates one replicate datasets. number replicates set nsim, seed can supplied reproducibility:

title: Simulation
text: Generating replicate involves two distinct sources randomness, simulate() accounts : Parameter uncertainty. fresh parameter vector drawn multivariate normal distribution implied estimates covariance matrix, using coef(mod) vcov(mod). represents uncertain fitted parameters . Observation noise. Given parameter draw, expected response μi\mu_i evaluated observation’s exposure covariates, response generated around . noise model family-appropriate: Bernoulli draws binomial, Poisson draws poisson, normal draws gaussian (), gamma draws Gamma. sources included, simulated val column behaves like genuine new dataset drawn fitted model, merely noiseless prediction.

title: Simulation
text: result tidy, long-format tibble nsim×\timesnobs rows: columns : dat_id – index identifying original observation (row data). sim_id – replicate row belongs (1 nsim). mu – expected response (response scale) observation sampled parameters. val – simulated response (mean plus family-appropriate noise). sampled coefficient values, prefixed coef_* (e.g. coef_`(Intercept)`, coef_aucss) avoid colliding predictor columns name, repeated across rows replicate – one parameter draw used per replicate. model’s predictor columns (aucss), carried along can group plot exposure covariates. Requesting several replicates stacks , sampled coefficients vary one replicate next staying constant within replicate:
code: names(sim1) #> [1] "dat_id"           "sim_id"           "mu"               "val"              #> [5] "coef_(Intercept)" "coef_aucss"       "aucss" sims <- simulate(mod, nsim = 50, seed = 1) dim(sims) #> [1] 15000     7  # one parameter draw per replicate unique(sims[sims$sim_id <= 3, c("sim_id", "coef_(Intercept)", "coef_aucss")]) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   sim_id `coef_(Intercept)` coef_aucss #>    <int>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1      1              -1.72    0.00190 #> 2      2              -1.74    0.00202 #> 3      3              -1.61    0.00176

title: Simulation
text: natural use replicates predictive check: model adequate, distribution simulated responses resemble distribution observed response. Overlaying density observed biomarker_change densities several simulated replicates gives quick visual check:  observed distribution sits comfortably within spread simulated replicates, ’d hope see well-fitting model.
code: ggplot(sims, aes(val, group = sim_id)) +   geom_line(stat = "density", colour = "steelblue", alpha = 0.3) +   geom_line(     aes(biomarker_change),     data = erglm_data,     stat = "density",     inherit.aes = FALSE,     colour = "black",     linewidth = 1   ) +   labs(     x = "Biomarker change from baseline",     y = "Density",     subtitle = "Observed (black) vs 50 simulated replicates (blue)"   )

title: Simulation
text: simulate() bundles parameter sampling noise generation together, erglm_fun() exposes deterministic core: prediction function . returns function two arguments, param data, default values used model fitted: control arguments, can evaluate model situations original fit never saw. Supplying param lets ask counterfactual “parameters different?” questions – example, setting intercept zero: Supplying data lets evaluate curve exposures covariate values choosing – instance, tracing exposure-response relationship grid aucss values: default erglm_fun() returns predictions response scale (type = "response"); pass type = "link" link scale.

title: Simulation
text: erglm_fun() tool reach simulate() doesn’t exactly need want assemble pieces . illustration, can build exposure-response curve parameter-uncertainty band combining erglm_fun() manual parameter draw – reproducing, lower level, parameter-sampling step simulate() performs internally. recipe : draw many parameter vectors estimated sampling distribution, evaluate curve exposure grid draw, summarise resulting family curves pointwise.  band reflects parameter uncertainty, summarised mean curve never added residual noise – ’s analogue confidence band. Adding step draws rnorm(..., sd = sqrt(summary(mod)$dispersion)) around evaluated mean turn prediction band also captures observation noise, precisely extra ingredient simulate() supplies . essential trade-two tools: simulate() convenient complete, erglm_fun() transparent fully control.
code: set.seed(1) n_draws <- 500 draws <- mvtnorm::rmvnorm(n_draws, mean = coef(mod), sigma = vcov(mod)) colnames(draws) <- names(coef(mod))  curve_grid <- tibble(aucss = seq(0, max(erglm_data$aucss), length.out = 100))  # evaluate the curve for every parameter draw curves <- apply(draws, 1, function(p) f(data = curve_grid, param = p))  # summarise pointwise across draws band <- tibble(   aucss = curve_grid$aucss,   fit = f(data = curve_grid),   lwr = apply(curves, 1, stats::quantile, probs = 0.025),   upr = apply(curves, 1, stats::quantile, probs = 0.975) )  ggplot(band, aes(aucss)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lwr, ymax = upr), fill = "firebrick", alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(aes(y = fit), colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 1) +   labs(x = "AUCss", y = "Mean biomarker change", subtitle = "Point estimate with 95% parameter-uncertainty band")

what: The same tools across other glm() families
title: Simulation
text: tools work unchanged families erglm supports; noise model scale differ. binomial model, mu fitted probability val 0/1 outcome drawn Bernoulli(μ)\text{Bernoulli}(\mu): Poisson model, val drawn Poisson(μ)\text{Poisson}(\mu) always non-negative integer: Everything else – custom parameter values, custom data grids, building simulations top prediction function – carries exactly gaussian case.
code: mod_b <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = binomial())  sim_b <- simulate(mod_b, nsim = 2, seed = 1) sort(unique(sim_b$val)) #> [1] 0 1  f_b <- erglm_fun(mod_b) range(f_b()) #> [1] 0.1234 1.0000 mod_p <- erglm_model(ae_count ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = poisson())  sim_p <- simulate(mod_p, nsim = 2, seed = 1) all(sim_p$val == round(sim_p$val)) && all(sim_p$val >= 0) #> [1] TRUE

title: Simulation
text: erglm_vpc_sim() thin, VPC-shaped wrapper around simulate(): drops sampled coefficients mu column, splices simulated response back column response variable’s name, producing data frame shape expected erplots::er_vpc_plot(): Reach simulate() want full simulation detail (sampled parameters, expected simulated response); reach erglm_vpc_sim() just want VPC-ready data set hand plotting function.
code: sim <- erglm_vpc_sim(mod_b, nsim = 20, seed = 1234) sim #> # A tibble: 6,000 × 5 #>      ae1 aucss sex    row_id sim_id #>    <int> <dbl> <fct>   <int>  <int> #>  1     1  673. Male        1      1 #>  2     1 2806. Female      2      1 #>  3     1    0  Female      3      1 #>  4     1 1169. Female      4      1 #>  5     1  377. Male        5      1 #>  6     0  327. Female      6      1 #>  7     0    0  Male        7      1 #>  8     1 1208. Female      8      1 #>  9     0    0  Male        9      1 #> 10     0  254. Female     10      1 #> # ℹ 5,990 more rows

title: Simulation
text: simulate() erglm_fun()’s uncertainty workflows draw parameters mvtnorm, must installed. simulate() captures two sources variability (parameter uncertainty observation noise); band built erglm_fun() captures whatever choose include, decide deliberately whether want confidence-style band (means ) prediction-style band (means plus residual noise). quick analytic interval mean, prefer erglm_predict() predict(..., se.fit = TRUE), described modelling methods articles. Reach simulate() erglm_fun() need replicate datasets bespoke simulation logic. glm() families currently supported simulate()/erglm_vpc_sim() raise informative error rather silently falling back expectation-draw.

title: Authors and Citation

title: Authors and Citation
text: Navarro D (2026). erglm: Exposure-Response Tools GLM-Based Models. R package version 0.2.0.9000, https://github.com/djnavarro/erglm.
code: @Manual{,   title = {erglm: Exposure-Response Tools for GLM-Based Models},   author = {Danielle Navarro},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.2.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/djnavarro/erglm}, }

title: Exposure-Response Tools for GLM-Based Models
text: Provides estimation tools exposure-response models based glm(). mostly intended convenience package: core tools wrappers around glm(), tested supported binomial, poisson, gaussian, gamma families. plotting exposure-response models (including fitted erglm), see companion package erplots, supplies model-agnostic mini-language building exposure-response plots.

title: Exposure-Response Tools for GLM-Based Models
text: can install development version erglm like :
code: pak::pak("djnavarro/erglm")

title: Exposure-Response Tools for GLM-Based Models
code: library(erglm) library(tibble)  erglm_data #> # A tibble: 300 × 13 #>       id sex      age weight  dose treatment aucss cmaxss   ae1   ae2 ae_count #>    <int> <fct>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <fct>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <int> #>  1     1 Male      35     79   200 Drug       673.   97.3     0     1        1 #>  2     2 Female    22     58   200 Drug      2806.  301.      1     1        6 #>  3     3 Female    28     58     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        1 #>  4     4 Female    18     57   100 Drug      1169.  198.      1     1        0 #>  5     5 Male      28     77   100 Drug       377.   51.4     0     0        0 #>  6     6 Female    19     76   200 Drug       327.   25.4     1     0        0 #>  7     7 Male      30     70     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        0 #>  8     8 Female    34     60   100 Drug      1208.  133.      1     1        1 #>  9     9 Male      21     89     0 Placebo      0     0       0     0        0 #> 10    10 Female    34     56   200 Drug       254.   31.0     0     0        1 #> # ℹ 290 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: biomarker_change <dbl>, ae_duration <dbl>  mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss   #>   -1.791383     0.005497   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:       402.1  #> Residual Deviance: 193.4     AIC: 197.4

code: mod1 <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + sex + dose, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod2 <- erglm_scm_backward(mod1, candidates = c("sex", "dose")) #> Using seed = 9353 erglm_scm_history(mod2) #> # A tibble: 4 × 11 #>   iteration attempt step       action term_tested model_tested   model_converged #>       <int>   <int> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>          <lgl>           #> 1         0       0 base model <NA>   <NA>        ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 2         1       1 backward   remove ~dose       ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 3         1       2 backward   remove ~sex        ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 4         2       3 backward   remove ~sex        ae1 ~ aucss    TRUE            #> # ℹ 4 more variables: term_p_value <dbl>, model_aic <dbl>, model_bic <dbl>, #> #   model_updated <int>

what: Prediction function for an exposure-response model — erglm_fun
title: Prediction function for an exposure-response model — erglm_fun
text: Prediction function exposure-response model

title: Prediction function for an exposure-response model — erglm_fun
code: erglm_fun(object)

title: Prediction function for an exposure-response model — erglm_fun
text: object erglm model, returned erglm_model()

title: Prediction function for an exposure-response model — erglm_fun
text: function arguments param, data, type. param argument vector coefficients; defaults coef(object) (fitted coefficients) supplied. data argument data frame tibble; defaults object$data (data model fitted ) supplied. type argument string indicating type prediction generate (defaults "response") Takes fitted glm object input returns function evaluate underlying structural model user-specified parameters data (e.g., VPCs counterfactual simulation scenarios). Uses stats::family(object)$linkinv, works glm() family, just binomial/logistic models; tested binomial, poisson, gaussian, Gamma families. Named erglm_fun() consistency companion emaxnls package's emax_fun(), serves purpose emaxnls/emaxlogistic models.

title: Prediction function for an exposure-response model — erglm_fun
code: mod1 <- erglm_model(ae2 ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod1_fun <- erglm_fun(mod1)  # no arguments: reproduces the fitted model's own predictions p1 <- mod1_fun() p2 <- unname(predict(mod1, type = "response")) # same result  # user modifies the data set erglm_data2 <- erglm_data[1:20, ] p3 <- mod1_fun(data = erglm_data2)  p4 <- unname(predict(mod1, newdata = erglm_data2, type = "response")) # same result  # user modifies the parameters par2 <- coef(mod1) int1 <- par2["(Intercept)"] par2["(Intercept)"] <- 0 p5 <- mod1_fun(param = par2)

what: Link and inverse-link functions for a fitted model — erglm_link
title: Link and inverse-link functions for a fitted model — erglm_link
text: Every glm() family already carries link inverse-link functions (stats::family(mod)$linkfun / stats::family(mod)$linkinv), many users realise available taking. erglm_link() erglm_invlink() thin, discoverable wrappers around : erglm_link() maps response scale linear predictor scale, erglm_invlink() maps linear predictor scale back response scale.

title: Link and inverse-link functions for a fitted model — erglm_link
code: erglm_link(mod)  erglm_invlink(mod)

title: Link and inverse-link functions for a fitted model — erglm_link
text: mod fitted model, typically erglm_model/glm object.

title: Link and inverse-link functions for a fitted model — erglm_link
text: function one numeric-vector argument.

title: Link and inverse-link functions for a fitted model — erglm_link
code: mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = binomial()) erglm_link(mod)(0.5) #> [1] 0 erglm_invlink(mod)(0) #> [1] 0.5 erglm_link(mod)(erglm_invlink(mod)(-2:2)) #> [1] -2 -1  0  1  2

title: Fit an exposure-response model based on glm() — erglm_model
text: formula Model formula data Data set family error distribution link function use, stats::glm(). Defaults stats::gaussian(), matching stats::glm()'s default. Tested officially supported binomial(), poisson(), gaussian(), Gamma(); glm() families work generic mechanisms untested. ... arguments passed glm()

title: Fit an exposure-response model based on glm() — erglm_model
text: glm object

title: Fit an exposure-response model based on glm() — erglm_model
text: returned object class c("erglm_model", "glm", "lm"): glm object, little extra metadata attached. means usual glm/lm methods work unchanged, without needing erglm-specific equivalent – e.g. summary(), coef(), vcov(), confint(), predict(), AIC(), BIC(), logLik(), anova() comparing nested models. See vignette("methods", package = "erglm") worked examples . erglm_predict() separate, erglm-specific alternative predict() returns confidence intervals response scale tidy data frame; two complementary, competing.

title: Fit an exposure-response model based on glm() — erglm_model
code: mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss   #>   -1.791383     0.005497   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:	    402.1  #> Residual Deviance: 193.4 	AIC: 197.4  # other glm() families are also supported mod_pois <- erglm_model(ae_count ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = poisson()) mod_pois #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = family, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)        aucss   #>   -1.003955     0.001044   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:	    868.8  #> Residual Deviance: 275.6 	AIC: 713.8

title: Predictions and confidence intervals for exposure-response models — erglm_predict

See also erglm_fun() generating predictions arbitrary (possibly counterfactual) parameters data. tidy, opinionated alternative calling base R's predict() directly object – since object genuine glm object, predict() (predict(object, se.fit = TRUE), function based) work unchanged remain useful quick point estimates tidy data frame needed. See vignette("methods", package = "erglm") side--side comparison inherited glm/lm methods (summary(), vcov(), AIC(), etc.).

what: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
title: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
text: Stepwise covariate modelling exposure-response models

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
code: erglm_scm_forward(   mod,   candidates,   threshold = 0.01,   test = c("auto", "Chisq", "F"),   seed = NULL )  erglm_scm_backward(   mod,   candidates,   threshold = 0.001,   test = c("auto", "Chisq", "F"),   seed = NULL )  erglm_scm_history(mod)

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
text: mod erglm model object candidates Character vector list candidate terms threshold Threshold test test significance test use comparing nested models. "auto" (default) picks likelihood-ratio chi-squared test ("Chisq") families known dispersion (binomial, poisson) F-test ("F") families estimated dispersion parameter (gaussian, Gamma, inverse.gaussian, quasi*), matching stats::anova()'s test argument. Set explicitly override. seed Optional seed control order term tests

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
text: erglm_scm_forward() erglm_scm_backward(), updated erglm model returned, SCM history log updated internally. erglm_scm_history(), data frame returned containing SCM history log

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
text: seed exists safety measure two hypothetical sources run--run variation: () order candidate terms tested within step, (b) part model-fitting machinery secretly depending .Random.seed. currently implemented, () real, even effect usually invisible. Concretely: step erglm_scm_forward()/ erglm_scm_backward() shuffles candidate terms (sample()) testing one time, shuffled order thing seed (via withr::with_seed()) controls. Term p-values come stats::anova() models fitted stats::glm(), deterministic algorithm (iteratively reweighted least squares, random starting values) – candidate found best depend seed. seed can change candidate selected (rare, essentially measure-zero continuous predictors) case exact tie p-values within step, since ties broken encounter order (p_val < lowest_p/ p_val > highest_p strict inequalities internal .erglm_once_forward()/.erglm_once_backward() helpers). short: typical data, seed redundant reproducibility result (though still affects row order intermediate attempts recorded erglm_scm_history()) – retained mainly guard future refactors reintroducing genuine seed-sensitivity (e.g. candidate order ever used early-stopping rule rather exhaustively tested every step).

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for exposure-response models — erglm_scm
code: mod0 <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod1 <- erglm_scm_forward(mod0, candidates = c("sex", "dose")) erglm_scm_history(mod1) #> # A tibble: 3 × 11 #>   iteration attempt step       action term_tested model_tested   model_converged #>       <int>   <int> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>          <lgl>           #> 1         0       0 base model NA     NA          ae1 ~ aucss    TRUE            #> 2         1       1 forward    add    ~sex        ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 3         1       2 forward    add    ~dose       ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> # ℹ 4 more variables: term_p_value <dbl>, model_aic <dbl>, model_bic <dbl>, #> #   model_updated <int>  mod2 <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss + sex + dose, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod3 <- erglm_scm_backward(mod2, candidates = c("sex", "dose")) erglm_scm_history(mod3) #> # A tibble: 4 × 11 #>   iteration attempt step       action term_tested model_tested   model_converged #>       <int>   <int> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>          <lgl>           #> 1         0       0 base model NA     NA          ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 2         1       1 backward   remove ~dose       ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 3         1       2 backward   remove ~sex        ae1 ~ aucss +… TRUE            #> 4         2       3 backward   remove ~sex        ae1 ~ aucss    TRUE            #> # ℹ 4 more variables: term_p_value <dbl>, model_aic <dbl>, model_bic <dbl>, #> #   model_updated <int>

what: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
title: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
text: Add remove single covariate term existing erglm model, returning new fitted model object.

title: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
code: erglm_add_term(mod, term, quiet = FALSE)  erglm_remove_term(mod, term, quiet = FALSE)

title: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
text: mod erglm model object, returned erglm_model() term one-sided formula naming term add/remove, e.g. ~ sex quiet TRUE, suppress warning issued term added/removed (already model / model, respectively)

title: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
text: erglm model object. term added/removed (see quiet), original mod returned unchanged.

title: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
text: functions typically called directly; underpin erglm_scm_forward() erglm_scm_backward(). Named shaped match companion emaxnls package's emax_add_term()/emax_remove_term(), serve purpose emaxnls/emaxlogistic models – one structural difference: emaxnls's terms two-sided formulas naming structural parameter (e.g. E0 ~ AGE), since covariates attach specific Emax parameter, whereas erglm's terms plain one-sided glm() formula terms (e.g. ~ sex), since erglm equivalent parameter-level structure attach covariates .

title: Add or remove a covariate term from an exposure-response model — erglm_term
code: mod <- erglm_model(ae1 ~ aucss, erglm_data, family = binomial()) mod2 <- erglm_add_term(mod, ~ sex) mod3 <- erglm_remove_term(mod2, ~ sex)

what: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
title: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
text: VPC simulations exposure-response models

title: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
code: erglm_vpc_sim(object, nsim = 100, seed = NULL)

title: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
text: object erglm model, returned erglm_model() nsim Number replicates seed RNG state

title: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
text: data frame tibble. Contains one row per observation per simulated replicate (identified sim_id column), response variable replaced simulated value parameter uncertainty.

title: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
text: thin, VPC-shaped wrapper around simulate.erglm_model() (.e. simulate(object, ...)): parameter uncertainty reflected sampling coefficients model's asymptotic sampling distribution computing expected response coefficients; family-appropriate residual noise added top expectation produce full predictive draw (Bernoulli draws binomial, Poisson draws poisson, normal draws gaussian, gamma draws Gamma). dispersion parameter used noise single point estimate (summary(object)$dispersion), resampled per replicate. glm() families currently supported raise error. Unlike simulate(), sampled coefficients expected response (mu) dropped, simulated response (val) spliced back response column's original name – data frame shape expected erplots::er_vpc_plot() visualising result (e.g. VPC-style plot comparing observed simulated response rates).

title: VPC simulations for exposure-response models — erglm_vpc_sim
code: mod <- erglm_model(ae2 ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = binomial()) sim <- erglm_vpc_sim(mod) #> Using seed = 7603. Pass `seed = 7603` to reproduce this result. sim #> # A tibble: 30,000 × 5 #>      ae2 aucss sex    row_id sim_id #>    <int> <dbl> <fct>   <int>  <int> #>  1     1  673. Male        1      1 #>  2     1 2806. Female      2      1 #>  3     0    0  Female      3      1 #>  4     1 1169. Female      4      1 #>  5     0  377. Male        5      1 #>  6     0  327. Female      6      1 #>  7     0    0  Male        7      1 #>  8     1 1208. Female      8      1 #>  9     0    0  Male        9      1 #> 10     0  254. Female     10      1 #> # ℹ 29,990 more rows  mod_pois <- erglm_model(ae_count ~ aucss + sex, erglm_data, family = poisson()) erglm_vpc_sim(mod_pois) #> Using seed = 1484. Pass `seed = 1484` to reproduce this result. #> # A tibble: 30,000 × 5 #>    ae_count aucss sex    row_id sim_id #>       <int> <dbl> <fct>   <int>  <int> #>  1        1  673. Male        1      1 #>  2        7 2806. Female      2      1 #>  3        0    0  Female      3      1 #>  4        1 1169. Female      4      1 #>  5        0  377. Male        5      1 #>  6        0  327. Female      6      1 #>  7        0    0  Male        7      1 #>  8        0 1208. Female      8      1 #>  9        0    0  Male        9      1 #> 10        1  254. Female     10      1 #> # ℹ 29,990 more rows

what: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
title: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
text: Generates simulated response datasets fitted erglm model, propagating uncertainty parameter estimates. Useful simulation-based confidence bands, predictive checks, bootstrapping downstream analyses. Implements standard stats::simulate() generic, called simulate(object, ...) rather erglm-specific function name.

title: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
code: # S3 method for class 'erglm_model' simulate(object, nsim = 1, seed = NULL, ...)

title: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
text: object erglm model, returned erglm_model() nsim Number replicates seed Used set RNG seed. NULL, random seed chosen reported. ... Ignored

title: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
text: tibble one row per observation per simulated replicate, containing: dat_id, sim_id: identifiers original observation simulation replicate mu: expected response (response scale) sampled parameter vector val: simulated response value (mu plus family-appropriate noise) one coef_* column per model coefficient (e.g. coef_`(Intercept)`, coef_aucss), giving sampled parameter values used replicate – prefixed avoid colliding predictor columns name model's predictor columns (including response)

title: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
text: Samples new parameter values multivariate normal distribution implied model's variance-covariance matrix (via mvtnorm::rmvnorm()), evaluates expected response sampled parameter vector using erglm_fun(), draws simulated response prediction using family-appropriate residual noise (.erglm_draw_response() mechanism used erglm_vpc_sim(): Bernoulli draws binomial, Poisson draws poisson, normal draws gaussian, gamma draws Gamma). dispersion parameter used noise single point estimate (summary(object)$dispersion), resampled per replicate. glm() families currently supported raise informative error. erglm_vpc_sim() thin wrapper around method: calls simulate() internally, drops sampled coefficients mu splices simulated response (val) back response column's original name, produce VPC-ready data set. Use simulate() directly want full simulation detail (sampled parameters, expected simulated response, one row per observation per replicate); use erglm_vpc_sim() just want VPC-shaped data set.

title: Simulate responses from an exposure-response model — simulate.erglm_model
